one_line_takeaway: ARMOR shows that orchestrating classical white-box attacks (CW, JSMA, STA) with VLM/LLM agents that reason about image semantics and adaptively reparameterize/mix perturbations roughly doubles black-box transfer success (ASR 0.396 vs. next-best 0.196) against a blind ViT deepfake detector, at the cost of reduced perceptual similarity (SSIM 0.701) versus static baselines.
key_finding: On the blind ViT-B/16 target, ARMOR achieves ASR=0.396 (wASR=0.280, SSIM=0.701), the highest among all 12 compared attackers, versus the next-best baseline AutoAttack-PGD at ASR=0.196 -- roughly double. On the surrogates it attains perfect ASR=1.000 on both ResNet-50 and DenseNet-121 with high wASR (0.982 and 0.977 respectively). Transferability analysis (Table 2, restricted to attackers with psurr>0.5) shows ARMOR reaches psurr=1.000 and the highest conditional transfer probability pcond=0.396, described in the paper as 'over twice that of the next best method,' though its transfer drop delta-ASR=0.604 is not the smallest (TI-FGSM has the smallest delta-ASR=0.657 among losers but low absolute ptgt=0.151). Ablation (Table 3) shows removing learned orchestration (replacing the Mixer with uniform averaging) collapses ASR from 0.396 to 0.010; removing the InfoAgent further drops it to 0.006; removing both InfoAgent and ConductorAgent (reverting to static default hyperparameters) yields ASR=0.007, slightly above the InfoAgent-only-ablated variant -- the paper interprets this as evidence that 'unguided adaptation can reduce transfer,' i.e., reasoning must be coupled with reliable reparameterization rather than applied in isolation.
core_contribution: ARMOR, a multi-agent adversarial-attack orchestration framework that uses a Vision-Language Model (Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Instruct-AWQ) as an InfoAgent and an LLM (Qwen3-32B-AWQ) as ConductorAgent/AdvisorAgent/StrategistAgent to coordinate three canonical white-box adversarial primitives (Carlini-Wagner/CW, JSMA, and Spatially Transformed Attacks/STA) running in parallel against deepfake detectors. The agents run a closed-loop 'Mixing Desk' that: (1) sets a global objective (max target-class probability subject to an l-infinity budget epsilon and a minimum SSIM threshold tau) from VLM-derived semantic image analysis; (2) generates parallel per-method perturbations reparameterized by an AdvisorAgent between iterations; (3) blends the three perturbations via a weight vector optimized by randomized hill climbing over a score function that trades off black-box success, surrogate-ensemble transfer stability, and an SSIM quality penalty; and (4) detects optimization stagnation over a sliding window and escalates (relaxes tau, raises epsilon) to escape local plateaus. Output is a blended adversarial image for blind (black-box) targets, or the best single/blended attack selected by a confidence-and-SSIM score for white-box targets.
threat_model: Transfer-based black-box evasion attack against deepfake/synthetic-media detectors. The attacker has white-box access (gradients) to two surrogate classifiers, ResNet-50 and DenseNet-121, but no access to the actual target classifier, a Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16), which is treated as a blind black-box model. Perturbations are crafted on the surrogates under a shared l-infinity budget (epsilon = 8/255) and a common query limit (max 10k queries/image), then evaluated for their ability to flip the blind ViT-B/16's real/fake prediction from 'fake' to 'real' while remaining perceptually similar (measured via SSIM) to the original image.
limitations: The paper's own conclusion/results text notes: (1) the transfer-quality trade-off is explicit -- ARMOR's SSIM on the blind target (0.701) is markedly lower than most baselines (many of which report SSIM in the 0.7-0.97 range while achieving near-zero ASR), reflecting a deliberate sacrifice of perceptual fidelity for attack success; (2) despite being the best performer, absolute transfer remains limited -- ASR on the blind ViT is 0.396, meaning the majority of blind-target attacks still fail, and delta-ASR=0.604 quantifies a substantial residual generalization gap to unseen architectures; (3) the ablation shows adaptation without proper coupling (InfoAgent alone, case b) can perform worse than no adaptation at all (case c, static defaults), indicating the framework's benefit is fragile to which components are combined; (4) the paper's own 'Future work' section states intent to improve perceptual quality, broaden attack families, and study defenses using cross-agent signals -- implicitly acknowledging these as current gaps. Evaluation is restricted to a single dataset (AADD-LQ), a single blind target architecture (ViT-B/16), and two surrogate architectures (ResNet-50, DenseNet-121); generalization to other detector families or defenses (e.g., adversarial training, input purification) is not tested in the paper.

datasets_used: AADD benchmark, low-quality (LQ) fake subset -- 710 fake images from diverse content synthesis engines, resized to 224x224, ImageNet-normalized (mean=[0.485,0.456,0.406], std=[0.229,0.224,0.225])
quantitative_results: Blind ViT-B/16 target: ARMOR ASR=0.396±0.489, wASR=0.280±0.362, SSIM=0.701±0.170
Surrogate ResNet-50: ARMOR ASR=1.000±0.000, wASR=0.982±0.032, SSIM=0.982±0.032
Surrogate DenseNet-121: ARMOR ASR=1.000±0.000, wASR=0.977±0.039, SSIM=0.977±0.039
Transferability (psurr>0.5 subset): ARMOR psurr=1.000, ptgt=0.396, pcond=0.396, delta-ASR=0.604
Next-best blind-target ASR baseline: AutoAttack-PGD ASR=0.196±0.397 (also highest delta-ASR-relative ptgt among losers)

Ablation on blind ViT: Full ARMOR ASR=0.396/wASR=0.280/SSIM=0.701; Uniform Averaging (no orchestration) ASR=0.010/wASR=0.010/SSIM=0.959; Without InfoAgent (no reasoning) ASR=0.006/wASR=0.005/SSIM=0.810; Without InfoAgent+ConductorAgent (no reparam.) ASR=0.007/wASR=0.006/SSIM=0.803
Hyperparameters: CW iterations=1000, JSMA top-k=2, STA regularization=0.1, Mixer hill-climb iterations=500, max queries per image=10,000, common l-infinity budget epsilon=8/255
